info.name: MayaTrail API
info.description:
Complete API collection for the MayaTrail cloud-security simulation platform.

**Setup:**
1. Set the `base_url` collection variable to your server (default: http://localhost:8000).
2. Run "Auth / Login" — the `token` variable is set automatically via the test script.
3. All subsequent requests use `{{token}}` in the Authorization header.

**Enterprise endpoints** (Emulations folder) require the authenticated user to have `aws_role_arn` set on their account (i.e. `is_enterprise=True`).  Use POST /api/connectors/aws/verify/ to set it.

variable.description: Base URL of the MayaTrail backend. Change to http://localhost:80 when using docker-compose with nginx.
JWT access token. Set automatically by the Login and Google OAuth request test scripts.
JWT refresh token. Set automatically by the Login and Google OAuth request test scripts.
UUID of a Stack record. Set automatically by Deploy Emulation Stack; can also be set manually.
UUID of an EmulationRun record. Set automatically by the Trigger Attack request.
Emulation package name to use for emulation-specific requests.

item.name: Auth
item.description:
Authentication endpoints.

Registration flow: Register → Verify OTP → Login.
Password reset flow: Forgot Password → Reset Password → Login.
Alternative: Google OAuth (single step).

request.description:
Create a new (inactive) user account and send an OTP to the provided email address.

The user cannot log in until they verify the OTP via POST /api/auth/register/verify-otp/.

Fields:
- `invite_code` — optional; required only when REGISTRATION_INVITE_CODE is set in .env.
- `first_name` / `last_name` — optional but recommended.

Returns: 201 { message, email } on success.

Verify the OTP sent during registration and activate the user account.

Once verified, the user can call POST /api/auth/login/.

Returns: 200 { message } on success, 400 if the OTP is invalid or expired.

Generate and re-send a fresh OTP to the given email address.

Use this if the original OTP has expired or was not received.

Returns: 200 { message } on success.

Obtain a JWT access + refresh token pair.

The test script automatically stores both tokens in collection variables.
The user must have verified their email via OTP before this succeeds.

Returns: 200 { access, refresh } on success.

Exchange the stored refresh token for a new access token.

The test script updates the `token` collection variable automatically.

Returns: 200 { access } on success.

Return the authenticated user's profile.

Key fields to inspect:
- `is_enterprise` — must be true to use emulation endpoints
- `aws_role_arn` — set by POST /api/connectors/aws/verify/
- `is_demo` — set by POST /api/connectors/demo/
- `is_verified` — true once OTP is confirmed
- `auth_method` — 'password' or 'google'

Returns: 200 with user profile.

Blacklist the refresh token to invalidate the current session.

After calling this, both the access and refresh tokens are invalid.
The client must discard them from local storage.

Returns: 205 Reset Content on success.
Returns: 400 if the token is missing or already blacklisted.

Validate the reset OTP and set a new password.

OTP is sent by POST /api/auth/forgot-password/.

Returns: 200 { message } on success, 400 if OTP is invalid or expired.

Authenticate via a Google ID token and receive a MayaTrail JWT pair.

The `id_token` is obtained by the frontend after the user completes the Google sign-in consent screen via Google Identity Services.

No invite code or OTP verification is required for Google accounts.

Returns: 200 { access, refresh, user } on success.

item.name: Stacks
item.description:
Read-only stack state endpoints used during the emulation lifecycle.

All write operations (deploy, destroy, refresh, preview) are routed through the Emulations folder — POST /api/emulations/deploy/ creates and deploys an emulation stack in one step, and POST /api/emulations/{id}/destroy/ tears it down. The legacy write actions on /api/stacks/{id}/* require an `emulation_type` that cannot be set via the create endpoint, so they are not exposed here.

Keep this folder open while running the emulation flow to poll status and progress.

request.description:
List all stacks owned by the authenticated user.

Returns all stacks regardless of status, including destroyed and failed ones.

Retrieve a specific stack by UUID.

Use this to poll deployment status. The full status lifecycle for emulation stacks is:
`deploying → ec2_booting → ready_for_attack → attacking → attack_complete → destroying → (record deleted)`

Returns 404 once the stack is destroyed (record is deleted on successful destroy).

Poll live deployment progress for a stack currently being deployed.

Reads Celery task state from the Redis result backend and returns how many AWS resources have been created so far. The deploy task calls update_state(state='PROGRESS') every 2 Pulumi log lines.

**Response fields:**
- `resources_created` — number of Pulumi resources successfully created
- `total_resources` — expected total (19 for SCARLETEEL)
- `percentage` — 0–99 while deploying, 100 once the stack reaches a terminal state
- `recent_logs` — last 10 Pulumi output lines
- `status` — current Stack status

**Recommended polling interval:** every 3–5 seconds while `status == deploying`.
Stop polling when `percentage == 100` or `status` is no longer `deploying`.

Delete the stack database record only. Does NOT run `pulumi destroy`.

Use this to clean up orphaned DB records after manually destroying resources in AWS.

The stack is simply removed from the database — no cloud resources are touched.

item.name: Emulations (Enterprise)
item.description:
Enterprise APT emulation lifecycle.

**All endpoints require IsEnterpriseUser** — the authenticated user must have `aws_role_arn` set (call POST /api/connectors/aws/verify/ first).

Recommended test sequence:
1. List Emulations — verify SCARLETEEL is returned
2. Get Estimate — review cost before deploying (supports ?region= query param)
3. Get Techniques — verify MITRE data
4. Get Detections — verify SIGMA + KQL rules (12 total for SCARLETEEL)
5. Get Playbook — verify IR playbook content
6. Deploy Emulation Stack — starts the emulation infrastructure (sets stack_id variable)
7. Stacks / Stack Deployment Progress — watch resources_created count up to 19 (percentage 0→99)
8. Stacks / Get Stack — poll until status reaches ready_for_attack (deploying → ec2_booting → ready_for_attack)
9. Trigger Attack — starts the 6-phase attack; sets run_id variable
10. Poll Run Status — watch EmulationRun reach completed
11. Destroy Emulation Stack — tear down after testing

**Stuck stack recovery:** If the stack gets stuck in any non-terminal state (deploying, ec2_booting, attacking, failed), call Destroy Emulation Stack directly — it accepts any status except 'destroying'.

request.description:
List all registered emulation packages discovered from the emulations registry.

Returns camelCase fields matching the frontend Emulation TypeScript type.

Expected for SCARLETEEL:
- id: "scarleteel"
- name: "SCARLETEEL 2.0"
- severity: "CRITICAL"
- techniqueCount: 8
- schemaVersion: 1
- attackPath: 6 phases
- mitreMappings: 8 entries

query.description: AWS region to price for. Defaults to the Stack.region default (ap-south-1) if omitted.

request.description:
Return a cost breakdown for the emulation type before deployment.

Runs `pulumi preview --json` on the worker to enumerate exact resources, then prices them via the AWS Pricing API. Falls back to static MANIFEST figures if the live estimate fails or times out (60s).

**Response fields:**
- `source` — 'live-preview' (from AWS Pricing API) or 'manifest-fallback' (static)
- `resources` — per-resource cost breakdown array
- `costDrivers` — list of resource types that drive cost (live only)
- `warnings` — pricing warnings e.g. unknown instance type (live only)
- `resourceCount` — number of AWS resources to be created
- `totalCostPerHourUsd` — hourly cost
- `defaultTtlHours` — auto-destroy TTL (4 hours for SCARLETEEL)
- `estimatedTotalUsd` — totalCostPerHourUsd * defaultTtlHours

Optional `?region=` query param overrides the default region for pricing.

Return MITRE ATT&CK kill-chain phases and full technique mappings from the MANIFEST.

Expected for SCARLETEEL:
- attackPath: 6 phases (Initial Access → Persistence)
- mitreMappings: 8 entries including T1190, T1552.005, T1087.004, T1580, T1562.008, T1548.005, T1550.001, T1098
- techniqueCount: 8

exec: pm.test('Each rule has title and code', () => {

request.description:
Return SIGMA and KQL detection rules for the emulation.

Reads .yml (SIGMA) and .kql (KQL) files from the emulation's detections/ subdirectory.

Expected for SCARLETEEL:
- sigma: 6 rules (one per MITRE phase)
- kql: 6 rules (same techniques)
- totalCount: 12
- formats: "SIGMA (6) · KQL (6)"

Return the raw PLAYBOOK.md content for the emulation.

The `content` field contains the full markdown IR playbook. The frontend parses this into sections using the ## H2-section parser in platform.service.ts.

Returns 404 if no PLAYBOOK.md exists for the emulation type.
Returns 503 if EMULATIONS_BASE_DIR is not configured on the server.

exec: console.log('Stack ID:', body.stackId, '| Stack Name:', body.stackName);
console.log('Poll GET /api/stacks/{{stack_id}}/ for status transitions (deploying -> ec2_booting -> ready_for_attack).');
console.log('Conflict — active stack already exists. stackId:', body.stackId);

request.description:
Deploy an enterprise emulation stack.

Creates a Stack record with the given emulation_type, enqueues deploy_emulation_stack in the enterprise Celery queue, and stores the Celery task_id on the Stack record for progress polling.

Enforces one-active-stack-per-user — returns 409 with the existing `stackId` if the user already has a non-terminal emulation stack.

**Response (202):** { stackId, stackName }
**Response (409):** { detail, stackId } — existing active stack ID

The test script stores stackId in the `stack_id` collection variable.

exec: console.log('Poll GET /api/emulations/' + body.runId + '/ for attack progress.');
console.log('Conflict — stack is not in ready_for_attack status.');

request.description:
Trigger the 6-phase SCARLETEEL attack against a ready_for_attack emulation stack.

Creates an EmulationRun record and enqueues run_emulation_attack in the enterprise queue.

**Response (202):** { runId, stackId }
**Response (409):** stack is not in ready_for_attack status
**Response (404):** stack not found or not owned by caller

The test script stores runId in the `run_id` collection variable.

exec: console.log('Run status:', body.status, '| Phase:', body.phase_current + '/' + body.phase_total);
if (body.status === 'completed') console.log('Attack complete — 6 phases done. Proceed to Destroy Emulation Stack.');
if (body.status === 'failed') console.log('Attack FAILED — check stderr:', body.stderr);

request.description:
Poll the EmulationRun status after triggering the attack.

Run lifecycle: pending → running → completed (or failed)

**Response fields:**
- `status` — pending / running / completed / failed
- `phase_current` — current attack phase (1–6)
- `phase_total` — total phases (6 for SCARLETEEL)
- `stdout` — accumulated attack output
- `stderr` — error output if failed

Re-run until status is 'completed' or 'failed'.

Note: After a successful run, the Lambda backdoor created in Phase 6 is an attack-created resource not tracked by Pulumi state. It must be cleaned up separately.

exec: console.log('Destroy queued. Stack will transition: destroying -> (record deleted on success).');
console.log('Poll GET /api/stacks/{{stack_id}}/ until you receive a 404 (stack deleted) or status == failed.');

request.description:
Destroy an enterprise emulation stack via STS AssumeRole + pulumi destroy.

NOT blocked by busy statuses — can destroy stacks in any state (deploying, ec2_booting, ready_for_attack, attacking, attack_complete, failed). Useful for recovering stuck stacks.

Only blocked if the stack is already in DESTROYING state (returns 409).

**Response (202):** { detail: 'Destroy queued.', stackId }
**Response (409):** stack already being destroyed
**Response (404):** stack not found or not owned by caller

On success, the Stack DB record is deleted. Polling GET /api/stacks/{{stack_id}}/ will return 404 once the destroy completes.

item.name: Connectors
item.description:
AWS role connector verification and demo mode activation.

Connector flow:
- Enterprise path: POST /api/connectors/aws/verify/ with a role ARN
- Demo path: POST /api/connectors/demo/ (one-time activation, irreversible)

request.description:
Validate an AWS IAM role ARN via STS AssumeRole.

The server assumes the given role using its own IAM credentials — the request body only requires the role ARN. No AWS access keys should be sent.

On success:
- Sets `aws_role_arn` on the user profile
- Sets `is_verified=True` and `is_enterprise=True`
- Sets `is_demo=False`

**Response (200):** { status: 'verified', account_id: '...' }
**Response (400):** invalid ARN format
**Response (422):** STS AssumeRole failed (role not assumable — check trust policy)

Prerequisite: the IAM role must have a trust policy allowing the MayaTrail server's IAM principal.

Switch the authenticated user to demo mode.

Demo mode can only be activated once per user — subsequent calls return 409 DEMO_ALREADY_USED.

On success:
- Sets `is_demo=True`
- Sets `is_verified=False`
- Clears `aws_role_arn`
- Records `demo_activated_at` for TTL enforcement

**Response (200):** { status: 'ok', is_demo: true }
**Response (409):** { code: 'DEMO_ALREADY_USED', detail: '...' }

item.name: Logs
item.description: Read-only audit log entries for stacks and emulation runs.

request.description:
Return all audit log entries for the authenticated user's stacks and emulation runs.

Log entries are created automatically by the system on deploy, destroy, attack, and other lifecycle events.

Retrieve a single audit log entry by UUID.

Note: The `run_id` variable is used here as a convenience since log entry IDs are UUIDs. Replace with the actual log entry UUID if different from the current run_id.
